title: Introduction to Basic Syntax in Java
body:
Java programs are made up of **classes** containing **methods**. Every statement ends with a semicolon. Java is case-sensitive.

A basic Java program has:
- A `package` declaration (optional)
- `import` statements
- A `class` with the same name as the file
- A `main` method as the entry point

examples.label: Minimal Java program

title: Introduction to Java Primitives
body:
Java has 8 built-in primitive types that hold simple values directly (not as objects):

| Type | Size | Example |
|------|------|---------|
| `byte` | 8-bit | `-128` to `127` |
| `short` | 16-bit | `-32768` to `32767` |
| `int` | 32-bit | `-2^31` to `2^31-1` |
| `long` | 64-bit | append `L` suffix |
| `float` | 32-bit decimal | append `f` suffix |
| `double` | 64-bit decimal | default decimal type |
| `char` | 16-bit Unicode | single quotes `'A'` |
| `boolean` | 1-bit | `true` or `false` |

examples.label: Declaring primitives

title: Java main() Method Explained
body:
The `main` method is the entry point of every Java application. The JVM looks for this exact signature to start execution.

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

- `public` — accessible by the JVM from outside the class
- `static` — can be called without creating an instance
- `void` — returns nothing
- `String[] args` — command-line arguments passed as strings

examples.label: Accessing command-line args

title: Control Structures in Java
body:
Java control structures direct the flow of execution.

**if/else** — runs a block when a condition is true:
```java
if (score >= 90) { ... } else { ... }
```

**Ternary operator** — compact single-expression conditional:
```java
String result = score >= 60 ? "Pass" : "Fail";
```

**switch** — matches a value against multiple cases. Java 14+ supports arrow syntax.

examples.label: if/else

title: A Guide to Java Loops
body:
Java has three loop types:

**for** — use when the number of iterations is known:
```java
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { ... }
```

**while** — use when iterations depend on a condition:
```java
while (condition) { ... }
```

**do-while** — always executes the body at least once:
```java
do { ... } while (condition);
```

Use `break` to exit a loop early and `continue` to skip to the next iteration.

title: Guide to Java Packages
body:
Packages group related classes and prevent naming conflicts. The `package` statement must be the first line of a file. Use `import` to access classes from other packages.

```java
package com.example.app;
import java.util.ArrayList;
```

Java's standard library is organized into packages:
- `java.lang` — auto-imported (String, Math, System)
- `java.util` — collections, utilities
- `java.io` — file and stream I/O
- `java.net` — networking

examples.label: Package and import

title: Pass-By-Value as a Parameter Passing Mechanism in Java
body:
Java is strictly **pass-by-value**. This means:

- For **primitives**: a copy of the value is passed. Changes inside the method do not affect the caller.
- For **objects**: a copy of the *reference* is passed. The method can mutate the object's state, but cannot make the caller's variable point elsewhere.

This is a common source of confusion — Java never passes objects by reference.

examples.label: Primitive pass-by-value

title: Varargs in Java
body:
Varargs (`...`) lets a method accept a variable number of arguments of the same type. Internally, Java treats the arguments as an array.

```java
public static int sum(int... numbers) { ... }
```

Rules:
- Only one varargs parameter per method
- Must be the last parameter
- Can be called with zero or more arguments
- Can also be called with an explicit array

examples.label: Varargs method

title: Guide to hashCode() in Java
body:
`hashCode()` returns an integer used to bucket objects in hash-based collections like `HashMap` and `HashSet`.

**The contract:**
- Objects that are `equals()` must return the same `hashCode()`
- Objects with the same `hashCode()` do *not* have to be `equals()` (hash collision)

The default `hashCode()` is based on memory address. Override it whenever you override `equals()`. IDEs and `Objects.hash()` can generate it for you.

examples.label: Default hashCode

title: Java Switch Statement

title: Guide to the Java forEach Loop
body:
The enhanced `for` (for-each) loop iterates over arrays and `Iterable` collections without managing an index.

```java
for (Type element : collection) { ... }
```

Benefits:
- Cleaner syntax, no off-by-one errors
- Works with any `Iterable` (List, Set, etc.)

Limitations:
- Cannot modify the iteration index
- Cannot remove elements safely (use `Iterator` instead)
- No access to the current index

examples.label: for-each over array

title: Immutable Objects in Java
body:
An immutable object cannot be modified after creation. Benefits: thread-safe by default, safe to share and cache, easier to reason about.

To make a class immutable:
1. Declare the class `final`
2. Make all fields `private final`
3. No setter methods
4. Defensive copies of mutable fields in the constructor

`String` is the most common example — every string operation returns a *new* String.

examples.label: Immutable class
